description: Trigger discrimination for the targets-vs-allocation seam: per-hop budget engineering (this skill) vs SLO/error-budget/alerting definition (slo-reliability-architect), measurement instruments (performance-test-harness), trace wiring (observability-operator), and per-hop reduction work (query-plan-reader).

cases.prompt: The 300ms p95 target for checkout is agreed. Now split it across gateway, auth, order service, payment call, and the database — with timeouts derived from the split and rules for anyone adding a new call to the path.
cases.reason: The target EXISTS ('agreed'); the ask is allocation — per-hop budgets, derived timeouts, and the budget-claim rule. That is the spend plan, not target-setting, measuring, or wiring.

cases.prompt: What latency should we actually promise users on checkout, how much error budget do we get, and at what burn rate does someone get paged?
cases.note: SEAM CASE (primary) — what-users-must-experience, error budgets, and paging are the SLO catalog. The budget architect CONSUMES the resulting target and allocates milliseconds to components; it never chooses the promise, the error budget, or what pages.
cases.reason: Target, error budget, and alerting design are reliability-target work — the seam's other side.

cases.prompt: Build the pre-release check that measures checkout latency percentiles against our numbers on every release candidate and fails the build on regression.
cases.note: SEAM CASE (designs-vs-measures) — the ask is the measurement INSTRUMENT: baselines, percentile comparison, regression gates in CI. Budgets are the thresholds it asserts; designing the instrument is D10 measurement work, not allocation.
cases.reason: Measurement harness construction, not deciding which hop may spend which milliseconds.

cases.prompt: Instrument the request path with spans on every hop and build the dashboard that shows where checkout time goes in production.
cases.reason: Span instrumentation and dashboards are live telemetry implementation — observability-operator's manual-invocation surface. The budget design specifies the annotation convention; the wiring is not its work.

cases.prompt: The database hop is spending 180ms of its 60ms allowance — here's the dominant query's plan. Get that hop back under its number.
cases.reason: A hop over budget with a plan in hand is reduction work owned by the layer skill (query-plan-reader here; caching or frontend skills for their layers). The budget architect decides how much the hop MAY spend — never how it gets there.
